paragraphs.text:
%md

# Visualization of User Behavior Data in Zeppelin

This note is used to record the visualization part of the e-commerce user behavior analysis. The dataset contains different actions made by users, such as viewing a product, adding it to the cart, saving it as a favourite, and making a purchase.

I used Zeppelin for this part because it can run SparkSQL directly and show the query results as charts. This makes it easier to check the data pattern while doing the analysis. The visualizations here will also be used to support the discussion in the final report.

msg.data:
Visualization of User Behavior Data in Zeppelin

This note is used to record the visualization part of the e-commerce user behavior analysis. The dataset contains different actions made by users, such as viewing a product, adding it to the cart, saving it as a favourite, and making a purchase.

I used Zeppelin for this part because it can run SparkSQL directly and show the query results as charts. This makes it easier to check the data pattern while doing the analysis. The visualizations here will also be used to support the discussion in the final report.

paragraphs.text:
%spark

import org.apache.spark.sql.functions._

val df = spark.read
  .option("header", "false")
  .option("inferSchema", "true")
  .csv("hdfs:///user/maria_dev/final_report/UserBehavior_200k.csv")
  .toDF("user_id", "item_id", "category_id", "behavior_type", "timestamp")

df.printSchema()
df.show(10)

%md

## Loading and Preparing the Data

The data was loaded from HDFS into Spark so that it could be queried using SparkSQL. Each row represents one user action on a product. The columns used in this analysis include user ID, item ID, category ID, behavior type, and timestamp.

Before creating the charts, I checked the structure of the data and prepared the time column. The timestamp was converted into hour information because user behavior may change at different times of the day. This step helps make the following visual analysis more meaningful.

msg.data:
Loading and Preparing the Data

The data was loaded from HDFS into Spark so that it could be queried using SparkSQL. Each row represents one user action on a product. The columns used in this analysis include user ID, item ID, category ID, behavior type, and timestamp.

Before creating the charts, I checked the structure of the data and prepared the time column. The timestamp was converted into hour information because user behavior may change at different times of the day. This step helps make the following visual analysis more meaningful.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Behavior Type Distribution

This chart gives a general view of the four behavior types in the dataset. It helps me understand which action appears most often before moving to more detailed analysis.

The result is useful because e-commerce users usually browse more than they purchase. By comparing page view, favourite, cart, and purchase actions, I can see the basic structure of user behavior in the dataset.

msg.data:
Behavior Type Distribution

This chart gives a general view of the four behavior types in the dataset. It helps me understand which action appears most often before moving to more detailed analysis.

The result is useful because e-commerce users usually browse more than they purchase. By comparing page view, favourite, cart, and purchase actions, I can see the basic structure of user behavior in the dataset.

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT 
  behavior_type,
  COUNT(*) AS total_actions
FROM user_behavior
GROUP BY behavior_type
ORDER BY total_actions DESC

msg.data:
behavior_type	total_actions
pv	179146
cart	11023
fav	5759
buy	4073

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Conversion Funnel Analysis

This part looks at the user behavior flow from product browsing to purchase. The four actions are arranged as page view, favourite, cart, and buy.

I used this query to show how many actions appear at each stage. This helps me see the drop from early product interest to final purchase. It is useful for the report because the project focuses on purchase conversion, not only total user activity.

msg.data:
Conversion Funnel Analysis

This part looks at the user behavior flow from product browsing to purchase. The four actions are arranged as page view, favourite, cart, and buy.

I used this query to show how many actions appear at each stage. This helps me see the drop from early product interest to final purchase. It is useful for the report because the project focuses on purchase conversion, not only total user activity.

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT 
  behavior_type,
  COUNT(*) AS action_count
FROM user_behavior
WHERE behavior_type IN ('pv', 'fav', 'cart', 'buy')
GROUP BY behavior_type
ORDER BY 
  CASE behavior_type
    WHEN 'pv' THEN 1
    WHEN 'fav' THEN 2
    WHEN 'cart' THEN 3
    WHEN 'buy' THEN 4
  END

msg.data:
behavior_type	action_count
pv	179146
fav	5759
cart	11023
buy	4073

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Preparing Time Features

In this step, I prepared the timestamp column for time-based analysis. The original dataset stores time as a Unix timestamp, so it is not easy to directly understand the hour of user activity.

I converted the timestamp into a readable datetime format and extracted the hour value. This makes it possible to study how user behavior changes during different hours of the day.

msg.data:
Preparing Time Features

In this step, I prepared the timestamp column for time-based analysis. The original dataset stores time as a Unix timestamp, so it is not easy to directly understand the hour of user activity.

I converted the timestamp into a readable datetime format and extracted the hour value. This makes it possible to study how user behavior changes during different hours of the day.
df_time: org.apache.spark.sql.DataFrame = [user_id: int, item_id: int ... 5 more fields]

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Hourly User Activity Trend

This section looks at how user behavior changes across different hours of the day. After preparing the timestamp column, I used the hour value to group user actions.

The four behavior types are separated into different columns. This makes the trend easier to compare because page view, cart, favourite, and purchase actions may not happen at the same level.

msg.data:
Hourly User Activity Trend

This section looks at how user behavior changes across different hours of the day. After preparing the timestamp column, I used the hour value to group user actions.

The four behavior types are separated into different columns. This makes the trend easier to compare because page view, cart, favourite, and purchase actions may not happen at the same level.

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT
  hour,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'pv' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS pv_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cart_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'fav' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS fav_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'buy' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS buy_count
FROM user_behavior_time
GROUP BY hour
ORDER BY hour

%md

## Top Product Categories by Purchase

This section looks at which product categories receive the highest number of purchase actions. Since the dataset records user behavior but does not include actual sales revenue, I used purchase count as a sales-related indicator.

This chart helps me identify where buying activity is more active in the dataset. It also supports the sales-related analysis in the final report.

msg.data:
Top Product Categories by Purchase

This section looks at which product categories receive the highest number of purchase actions. Since the dataset records user behavior but does not include actual sales revenue, I used purchase count as a sales-related indicator.

This chart helps me identify where buying activity is more active in the dataset. It also supports the sales-related analysis in the final report.

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT 
  category_id,
  COUNT(*) AS purchase_count
FROM user_behavior
WHERE behavior_type = 'buy'
GROUP BY category_id
ORDER BY purchase_count DESC
LIMIT 10

%md

## User-Level Behavior Features

This section summarizes user behavior at the user level. Instead of looking at each action separately, I counted how many times each user performed page view, favourite, cart, and purchase actions.

This step is useful because these counts can become behavioral features for later analysis. For example, a user with many page views and cart actions may show a different purchase tendency compared with a user who only views products.

msg.data:
User-Level Behavior Features

This section summarizes user behavior at the user level. Instead of looking at each action separately, I counted how many times each user performed page view, favourite, cart, and purchase actions.

This step is useful because these counts can become behavioral features for later analysis. For example, a user with many page views and cart actions may show a different purchase tendency compared with a user who only views products.

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT 
  user_id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'pv' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS pv_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'fav' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS fav_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'cart' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS cart_count,
  SUM(CASE WHEN behavior_type = 'buy' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS buy_count
FROM user_behavior
GROUP BY user_id
LIMIT 20
